SOURCE 2: MARIA NYAMAL - REFUGEE BECOMES TEACHER (8/10)

URL: https://www.rescue.org/announcement/critical-role-trained-and-committed-teachers-refugee-education-0
PUBLICATION: International Rescue Committee (IRC) - February 20, 2025
PROGRAM: TeachWell (LEGO Foundation, Grundfos Foundation)

==================================================================================
FULL NARRATIVE ARC
==================================================================================

South Sudan → moved to Kakuma Refugee Camp 2013 → age 24 → originally no interest in education → saw fellow South Sudanese refugees struggling to learn Swahili (essential language for academic success and social integration) → "something shifted" → realized she could make a difference → "If they see a [South Sudanese] refugee teaching [Swahili], they will believe it's not hard. They, too, can do it" → applied to Lutheran World Foundation to be teacher → offered one-year contract → "to her delight" → began teaching with no formal training → brief teacher-to-teacher orientation, classroom observation only → later joined TeachWell's Teacher Continuous Capacity Development (TCCD) pilot program (44 refugee teachers) → four-day training with Gladys Sadera's team → "Before, as a teacher, you did most of the work. We never knew that the learners could participate and do their part" → learned active, child-centered learning, interactive methods (games, songs, hands-on activities) → lesson observations, one-on-one coaching, teacher learning circles → now teaching 219 students in single classroom → TeachWell training gave her tools to improve engagement → "Most of these teachers very much look forward to these trainings because they rarely get them. Most projects focus so much on the learners—but no one ever thinks about the teachers" → enrolled in Diploma in Primary Teacher Education (DPTE) program → working toward teaching degree

==================================================================================
KEY EMOTIONAL MOMENTS (15+)
==================================================================================

1. Moved to Kakuma from South Sudan 2013
2. "Originally had no interest in education"
3. Saw fellow South Sudanese refugees struggling with Swahili
4. "Something shifted" (turning point)
5. Realized she could make a difference
6. "If they see a [South Sudanese] refugee teaching [Swahili], they will believe it's not hard"
7. "Motivated by her desire to help her community"
8. Applied to be teacher
9. "To her delight, offered a one-year contract"
10. Started teaching with no formal training
11. Brief orientation only (unprepared)
12. Selected for TeachWell pilot (44 teachers)
13. Four-day training "proved instrumental"
14. "We never knew that the learners could participate" (revelation)
15. Manages 219 students (daunting challenge)
16. Training made managing large classes easier
17. "Most projects focus so much on the learners—but no one ever thinks about the teachers" (advocacy)
18. Enrolled in DPTE (pursuing degree)

==================================================================================
DIRECT QUOTES
==================================================================================

"If they see a [South Sudanese] refugee teaching [Swahili], they will believe it's not hard. They, too, can do it." - Maria

"Before, as a teacher, you did most of the work. We never knew that the learners could participate and do their part." - Maria

"Most of these teachers very much look forward to these trainings because they rarely get them. Most projects focus so much on the learners—but no one ever thinks about the teachers." - Maria

"Controlling the learners is very hard. But teachers report that after the training it's now easier to manage big classes. For example, if a teacher creates an activity and calls a few learners to the front to demonstrate, the others become curious to understand what's happening. They work hard, hoping to be chosen next." - Gladys Sadera (trainer)

==================================================================================
ESL MULTIPLIERS (6/6)
==================================================================================

✓ BUILDING NEW LIFE: South Sudan → Kakuma → no interest in teaching → discovered purpose → became teacher → pursuing degree
✓ BELONGING: South Sudanese community in Kakuma, Lutheran World Foundation, TeachWell cohort (44 teachers), teacher learning circles
✓ CONNECTION ACROSS DIFFERENCES: South Sudanese teaching Swahili (bridge between communities), refugee teacher → refugee students
✓ OVERCOMING "NOT GOOD ENOUGH": No formal training → selected for pilot program → 219 students → pursuing teaching degree
✓ PERSISTENCE: No initial interest → saw need → applied → started untrained → sought training → manages huge class → pursuing degree
✓ FIRST-TIME COURAGE: First time considering teaching, first time applying, first classroom, first training, first time advocating for teachers

==================================================================================
RATING: 8/10
==================================================================================

STRENGTHS: Community motivation, cultural bridge (South Sudanese teaching Swahili), "something shifted" moment, 219 students (concrete challenge), teacher advocacy ("no one thinks about teachers"), professional development arc, TeachWell program details

WEAKNESSES: Less personal detail than other sources, shorter narrative, less dramatic arc, South Sudan backstory missing

WHY IT WORKS: Career discovery narrative, community service, cultural bridge, teacher's perspective (often missing), professional development emphasis, manageable class size as concrete detail

USAGE NOTES: Preserve "If they see a refugee teaching, they'll believe it's not hard" (cultural bridge), "something shifted" (turning point), 219 students (scale), "no one thinks about teachers" (advocacy), TeachWell training transformation
